metadata.reviewNote: Definitions and formal mathematics are textbook-first. Review sheets determine unit order and emphasis; generated practice remains labelled as generated.

sources.fileName: CO 250 W26 IP and NLP formulation review.pdf
A Gentle Introduction to Optimization (B. Guenin, J. Könemann, L. Tunçel) (Z-Library).pdf.pdf

units.title: LP formulation
units.summary: Turn a real decision into variables, an objective, and constraints.
units.whyItMatters: A solver can optimize only the mathematical model it receives. Good formulation makes the intended decision and every physical or logical limit explicit.

definitions.title: Linear program
definitions.formalStatement: An optimization problem whose objective and constraints are affine functions of the decision variables.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: Every variable appears only to the first power and variables are not multiplied together.
commonMistakes: Omitting sign restrictions
Using an undefined unit

procedures.name: Formulate a linear program

steps.title: Define decision variables
steps.detail: State what each variable measures, with units and indices.

steps.title: Write the objective
steps.detail: Express exactly what should be maximized or minimized.

steps.title: Translate every limit
steps.detail: Write resource, balance, linking, and sign constraints.

steps.title: Audit the model
steps.detail: Check units, directions, omitted meanings, and whether every symbol is defined.

checkpoints: Do the units match on both sides?
Could the model choose a physically meaningless value?
Is every intended condition encoded?
commonMistakes: Defining a variable but not enforcing its intended meaning
Reversing a capacity inequality
Forgetting nonnegativity

workedExample.title: Product mix
workedExample.prompt: A stand sells apples for $2/kg and bananas for $1.50/kg. It can hold 75 kg and has 4 m² of shelf space. Apples and bananas use 0.08 and 0.05 m²/kg.
steps: Let a and b be kilograms of apples and bananas.

commonMistakes: Treating a defined variable meaning as if the solver automatically knows it
Mixing kilograms and square metres in one coefficient
Writing revenue as a constraint instead of the objective

units.title: Integer & nonlinear formulation
units.summary: Model discrete choices, logical conditions, and nonlinear relationships explicitly.
units.whyItMatters: Binary and integer variables encode indivisible decisions; nonlinear expressions broaden what can be represented but generally make optimization harder.

definitions.title: Binary variable
definitions.formalStatement: A variable x constrained by x ∈ {0,1}.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It records whether a condition or choice is off or on.
commonMistakes: Using a binary variable without linking it to the continuous decision

sourceReferences.fileName: CO 250 W26 IP and NLP formulation review.pdf

procedures.name: Encode a logical condition
recognitionSignals: at most a number of types

steps.title: State the binary meaning
steps.detail: Write exactly what y=1 and y=0 mean.

steps.title: Derive both cases
steps.detail: Write the constraint that should hold in each binary state.

steps.title: Choose a justified bound
steps.detail: Use the smallest valid M when a big-M link is needed.

steps.title: Test y=0 and y=1
steps.detail: Substitute both values and verify the intended feasible sets.

checkpoints: Does the link force zero when the choice is off?
Is M valid and not needlessly huge?
failureCases: no finite valid bound is known
commonMistakes: Adding only a count of selected types without linking selection to quantity

sourceReferences.fileName: CO 250 W26 IP and NLP formulation review.pdf

commonMistakes: Assuming the computer knows the prose interpretation of a binary variable
Writing x≤M without multiplying by the binary variable

sourceReferences.fileName: CO 250 W26 IP and NLP formulation review.pdf

units.title: LP outcomes & certificates
units.summary: Distinguish optimal, infeasible, and unbounded LPs—and verify each result with a short certificate.
units.whyItMatters: Certificates turn a solver's claim into a calculation you can independently check.

definitions.title: Certificate
definitions.formalStatement: A compact object that permits efficient verification of a claimed LP outcome.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: Instead of trusting a solver, check a vector and a few matrix relations.
nonExamples: a solver status with no evidence
commonMistakes: Checking only part of the certificate conditions

theorems.name: LP outcome trichotomy
theorems.formalStatement: Every linear program is infeasible, unbounded, or has an optimal solution.
assumptions: the problem is a linear program
theorems.plainLanguageMeaning: There is no fourth solver outcome in the mathematical model.
theorems.whyItMatters: It organizes both algorithms and certificate checks.
commonMisuses: Treating a large feasible objective as proof of unboundedness

procedures.name: Verify an LP certificate
recognitionSignals: a candidate vector y or direction d is supplied

steps.title: Identify the claimed outcome
steps.detail: Optimal, infeasible, and unbounded certificates have different conditions.

steps.title: Check dimensions
steps.detail: Confirm every matrix-vector product is defined.

steps.title: Check every sign and equality
steps.detail: Do not infer one certificate condition from another.

steps.title: State the contradiction or ray
steps.detail: Explain why the verified relations prove the outcome.

checkpoints: Did you check feasibility of the base point for unboundedness?
Is cᵀd strictly positive for maximization?
commonMistakes: Checking Ad=0 but not d≥0
Using weak inequalities where a strict objective improvement is required
Confusing a bad candidate solution with infeasibility
Forgetting the feasible starting point in an unbounded ray

units.title: Standard equality form
units.summary: Convert LPs to equality constraints and nonnegative variables, then describe bases and canonical forms.
units.whyItMatters: Simplex operates on a standard representation and moves between bases.

definitions.title: Standard equality form
definitions.formalStatement: max cᵀx subject to Ax=b and x≥0.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: All constraints are equalities and every variable is nonnegative.
commonMistakes: Adding a slack variable with the wrong sign

definitions.title: Basic feasible solution
definitions.formalStatement: A basic solution whose entries are all nonnegative.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: Set nonbasic variables to zero, solve for basic variables, and retain the solution only if it is feasible.
commonMistakes: Assuming every basic variable is strictly positive

procedures.name: Convert an LP to SEF

steps.title: Normalize the objective
steps.detail: Convert minimization to maximization when required by the course convention.

steps.title: Replace inequalities
steps.detail: Add or subtract nonnegative slack variables.

steps.title: Replace unrestricted variables
steps.detail: Write a free variable as the difference of two nonnegative variables.

steps.title: Replace nonpositive variables
steps.detail: Negate a new nonnegative variable.

checkpoints: Is every final constraint an equality?
Is every final variable nonnegative?
commonMistakes: Forgetting to transform the objective along with the constraints
Confusing a basis with its basic solution
Assuming a basic variable cannot equal zero

units.title: Simplex algorithm
units.summary: Improve one basic feasible solution at a time until optimality or unboundedness is certified.
units.whyItMatters: Simplex links algebraic pivots, certificates, and the geometry of LPs.

theorems.name: Simplex optimality and unboundedness tests
theorems.formalStatement: In a feasible canonical maximization form, nonpositive objective coefficients certify optimality; if an entering column is nonpositive, the LP is unbounded.
assumptions: LP is in the course's canonical maximization convention
theorems.conclusion: The corresponding terminating outcome is certified.
theorems.plainLanguageMeaning: Either no nonbasic direction improves the objective, or an improving direction can grow without bound.
theorems.whyItMatters: It tells simplex when to stop.
theorems.proofIdea: Read the objective and constraints in canonical form.
commonMisuses: Declaring unboundedness from a nonpositive column that is not entering

procedures.name: Run a simplex iteration
recognitionSignals: feasible canonical form with a positive objective coefficient

steps.title: Choose an entering variable
steps.detail: Select a nonbasic variable with positive objective coefficient.

steps.title: Choose a leaving variable
steps.detail: Apply the minimum ratio test using positive entries in the entering column.

steps.title: Change the basis
steps.detail: Replace the leaving variable by the entering variable.

steps.title: Recompute canonical form
steps.detail: Pivot and repeat.

checkpoints: Did the ratio test ignore nonpositive column entries?
Is the new right-hand side nonnegative?
failureCases: no positive entry in the entering column implies unboundedness
commonMistakes: Using negative denominators in the ratio test
Applying the ratio test to every row
Choosing an entering variable with a nonpositive reduced cost

units.title: Two-phase simplex
units.summary: Use an auxiliary LP to find a feasible basis or certify that the original LP is infeasible.
units.whyItMatters: Simplex needs a starting BFS, and many LPs do not display one immediately.

theorems.name: Phase I feasibility test
theorems.formalStatement: The original LP is feasible exactly when the auxiliary LP has optimal value 0 under the course's negative-sum maximization convention.
assumptions: one auxiliary variable is added per equality after making right-hand sides nonnegative
theorems.conclusion: Zero yields a feasible solution; a negative optimum certifies infeasibility.
theorems.plainLanguageMeaning: Auxiliary variables can all be driven to zero exactly when the original equalities can be satisfied.
theorems.whyItMatters: It supplies simplex with a valid start or a certificate.
whenNotToUse: an evident feasible basis already exists
commonMisuses: Starting phase II while a positive auxiliary variable remains

procedures.name: Run two-phase simplex

steps.title: Make right-hand sides nonnegative
steps.detail: Multiply any row with negative right-hand side by -1.

steps.title: Add auxiliary variables
steps.detail: Create an identity basis.

steps.title: Solve phase I
steps.detail: Maximize the negative sum of auxiliary variables.

steps.title: Interpret the optimum
steps.detail: Zero gives an original feasible solution; negative means infeasible.

steps.title: Start phase II
steps.detail: Remove auxiliary variables and restore the original objective.

checkpoints: Were row sign changes tracked for certificates?
Are all auxiliary variables zero before phase II?
commonMistakes: Keeping the auxiliary objective in phase II
Forgetting to restore the original objective
Reading a negative phase-I optimum as unboundedness

units.title: Geometry of simplex
units.summary: See feasible regions as convex polyhedra and basic feasible solutions as extreme points.
units.whyItMatters: The geometry explains why simplex can search through vertices instead of every feasible point.

definitions.title: Convex set
definitions.formalStatement: A set S is convex when x,y∈S and λ∈[0,1] imply λx+(1−λ)y∈S.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: The entire line segment between any two points in the set stays in the set.
commonMistakes: Checking only the midpoint

theorems.name: BFS–extreme point equivalence
theorems.formalStatement: For P={x:Ax=b,x≥0}, a feasible point is an extreme point of P if and only if it is a basic feasible solution.
assumptions: P is in standard equality form
theorems.plainLanguageMeaning: The algebraic states visited by simplex are exactly the vertices of the feasible region.
theorems.whyItMatters: It connects simplex algebra to geometry.
whenToUse: testing an extreme point in SEF
whenNotToUse: outside the SEF representation without conversion
theorems.proofIdea: Tight nonnegativity constraints plus equality constraints characterize sufficient rank.
commonMisuses: Assuming every feasible point is basic

commonMistakes: Calling a point extreme because it lies on one tight constraint
Forgetting the rank requirement

units.title: Duality
units.summary: Pair every LP with a dual whose feasible solutions bound the primal objective.
units.whyItMatters: Duality supplies optimality certificates, sensitivity insight, and a second view of every LP.

theorems.name: Weak duality
theorems.formalStatement: For feasible x in max{cᵀx:Ax=b,x≥0} and feasible y in min{bᵀy:Aᵀy≥c}, cᵀx≤bᵀy. Equality makes both solutions optimal.
theorems.conclusion: The primal value is no greater than the dual value.
theorems.plainLanguageMeaning: Any dual feasible solution is an upper bound on every primal feasible solution.
commonMisuses: Claiming equality of optimal values from weak duality alone

theorems.name: Strong duality
theorems.formalStatement: A primal LP has an optimal solution if and only if its dual has an optimal solution, and then their optimal values are equal.
assumptions: the pair consists of primal and dual linear programs
theorems.conclusion: Existence of optima is paired and optimal values agree.
theorems.plainLanguageMeaning: When an LP has an optimum, the dual can meet it exactly.
theorems.whyItMatters: It guarantees an optimality certificate exists.
whenNotToUse: to claim both are feasible before an optimum is known
theorems.proofIdea: The course review derives the final dual solution from the terminal simplex basis.
commonMisuses: Assuming the converse of the unbounded-implies-infeasible corollary

procedures.name: Form the dual LP

steps.title: Match dimensions
steps.detail: Each primal constraint becomes a dual variable; each primal variable becomes a dual constraint.

steps.title: Transpose coefficients
steps.detail: Use Aᵀ in the dual constraints.

steps.title: Map signs and senses
steps.detail: Use the course conversion table for inequality directions and free variables.

steps.title: Check by dualizing again
steps.detail: The dual of the dual should recover the primal.

checkpoints: Does the dual objective use the primal right-hand side?
Are free-variable correspondences correct?
commonMistakes: Transposing dimensions incorrectly
Forgetting a free dual variable from a primal equality
Using weak duality without checking both solutions are feasible
Assuming both LPs cannot be infeasible

units.title: Complementary slackness
units.summary: Characterize primal-dual optimality by pairing positive variables with tight constraints.
units.whyItMatters: Complementary slackness often proves optimality and identifies unknown optimal solutions with less algebra.

theorems.name: Complementary slackness
theorems.formalStatement: For a feasible primal-dual pair, the two solutions are optimal if and only if each primal variable is zero or its dual constraint is tight, and each dual variable is zero or its primal constraint is tight.
assumptions: both solutions are feasible for a primal-dual LP pair
theorems.conclusion: All complementary slackness conditions hold exactly at optimality.
theorems.plainLanguageMeaning: Positive weight can be placed only against a constraint with no slack.
theorems.whyItMatters: It converts an equality of objective values into local zero-or-tight checks.
whenToUse: verify or solve for optimal primal-dual solutions

theorems.name: Farkas' lemma (course form)
theorems.formalStatement: Exactly one holds: there exists x with Ax=b,x≥0; or there exists y with Aᵀy≥0 and bᵀy<0.
assumptions: A and b define a standard-equality feasibility system
theorems.conclusion: The system is feasible or has an infeasibility certificate, but not both.
theorems.plainLanguageMeaning: Every infeasible SEF system has a separating linear certificate.
theorems.whyItMatters: It guarantees certificates of infeasibility exist.
whenNotToUse: with altered sign conventions unless converted
theorems.proofIdea: Apply duality to a feasibility LP.

procedures.name: Use complementary slackness

steps.title: Check primal and dual feasibility
steps.detail: CS never replaces feasibility.

steps.title: List positive variables
steps.detail: Force the corresponding opposite constraints to equality.

steps.title: List slack constraints
steps.detail: Force the corresponding opposite variables to zero.

steps.title: Solve and verify
steps.detail: Check every remaining CS condition.

checkpoints: Did you use inclusive OR?
Were all feasibility conditions checked?
commonMistakes: Using CS with infeasible candidates
alternativeMethods: match primal and dual objective values

commonMistakes: Treating zero-variable/tight-constraint as exclusive
Skipping feasibility

units.title: Integer programs
units.summary: Use LP relaxations and valid cuts to approach integral optimal solutions.
units.whyItMatters: Integrality makes models expressive and computationally difficult; relaxations provide bounds and algorithmic leverage.

definitions.title: LP relaxation
definitions.formalStatement: The linear program obtained from an integer program by removing its integrality restrictions.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It permits more points, so it is easier to solve and supplies a bound.
commonMistakes: Assuming a fractional relaxation optimum is feasible for the IP

procedures.name: Add a Gomory-style cutting plane
recognitionSignals: optimal LP basic solution has a fractional basic variable

steps.title: Solve the LP relaxation
steps.detail: If its optimum is integral, it is also optimal for the IP.

steps.title: Choose a fractional row
steps.detail: Use a canonical-form equation with fractional right-hand side.

steps.title: Floor the coefficients and right-hand side
steps.detail: Derive a valid inequality for integer solutions.

steps.title: Add the cut
steps.detail: Exclude the current fractional optimum and re-optimize.

checkpoints: Is the cut valid for every integer feasible point?
Does it exclude the current fractional point?
commonMistakes: Adding a constraint that removes an integer feasible point
Treating the LP bound as an integer feasible value
Rounding a fractional LP solution without checking constraints

units.title: Convex optimization
units.summary: Replace curved convex constraints by supporting halfspaces and certify optima with KKT conditions.
units.whyItMatters: Convexity preserves global structure: local supporting information can prove global optimality.

definitions.title: Convex function
definitions.formalStatement: f is convex when f(λa+(1−λ)b)≤λf(a)+(1−λ)f(b) for all a,b and λ∈[0,1].
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: The graph lies below each chord joining two graph points.
commonMistakes: Reversing the inequality

definitions.title: Subgradient
definitions.formalStatement: s is a subgradient of convex g at x̄ if g(x̄)+sᵀ(x−x̄)≤g(x) for every x.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It defines an affine function touching g at x̄ and lying below g everywhere.
commonMistakes: Using a local slope that crosses above g

theorems.name: KKT theorem (course convex form)
theorems.formalStatement: For min cᵀx subject to gᵢ(x)≤0 with convex differentiable gᵢ and a Slater point, a feasible x̄ is optimal iff −c lies in the cone generated by ∇gᵢ(x̄) over tight constraints.
theorems.conclusion: Optimality is equivalent to the stated cone condition.
theorems.plainLanguageMeaning: At an optimum, the direction opposing the objective is built from normals of the active constraints.
theorems.whyItMatters: It provides a checkable global optimality certificate for this convex NLP class.
whenToUse: linear objective with differentiable convex inequality constraints
whenNotToUse: without checking Slater's condition or convexity
theorems.proofIdea: Replace tight constraints by supporting halfspaces and apply the LP geometry of optimality.
commonMisuses: Using c instead of −c for the course's minimization form

procedures.name: Apply the course KKT condition
recognitionSignals: candidate optimum for differentiable convex constraints

steps.title: Verify convexity and differentiability
steps.detail: Check each constraint function.

steps.title: Find a Slater point
steps.detail: Exhibit a point satisfying every inequality strictly.

steps.title: Check candidate feasibility
steps.detail: Evaluate every constraint at x̄.

steps.title: Identify tight constraints
steps.detail: Keep only constraints equal to zero.

steps.title: Compute their gradients
steps.detail: Evaluate at x̄.

steps.title: Solve the cone equation
steps.detail: Express −c as a nonnegative combination of tight gradients.

checkpoints: Are all multipliers nonnegative?
Was −c used for a minimization objective?
failureCases: nonconvex or nondifferentiable constraints under this theorem version
commonMistakes: Including gradients of slack constraints
Applying KKT before checking its assumptions
Using gradients of non-tight constraints in the cone

questions.prompt: A bakery decides how many loaves of rye and sourdough to bake. State decision variables with units.
hints: What quantities can the bakery choose?
Include the unit in each definition.
solutionOutline: Define one variable per bread type.
questions.fullSolution: Let r be the number of rye loaves baked and s the number of sourdough loaves baked during the planning period. State r,s≥0; require integrality only if indivisibility is part of the model.
questions.finalAnswer: r = rye loaves; s = sourdough loaves.

commonWrongAnswers.explanation: Profit is derived from the decision; it is not the quantity chosen.

questions.prompt: A shop earns $5 per desk lamp and $3 per floor lamp. A desk lamp uses 2 assembly hours and a floor lamp uses 1 hour; 40 hours are available. Demand limits floor lamps to 24. Formulate an LP.
solutionOutline: Add time, demand, and nonnegativity constraints.
questions.fullSolution: Let d and f be the numbers of desk and floor lamps. Maximize 5d+3f subject to 2d+f≤40, f≤24, and d,f≥0.

commonWrongAnswers.explanation: Available hours are a capacity, so use ≤.

questions.prompt: A model defines y=1 when a machine is opened and x as its production, then uses only y∈{0,1}. Explain the missing condition if production is at most 500 when open.
questions.fullSolution: The prose meaning of y is not enforced. Add 0≤x≤500y. When y=0 this forces x=0; when y=1 it permits production up to 500.

commonWrongAnswers.explanation: This does not force x=0 when the machine is closed.

sourceReferences.fileName: CO 250 W26 IP and NLP formulation review.pdf

questions.prompt: Inventory I_t starts with I_0=10. Production p_t and demand d_t occur in period t. Write the inventory balance.
hints: Ending inventory equals starting inventory plus inflow minus outflow.
solutionOutline: Relate I_t to I_{t-1}, p_t, and d_t.
questions.fullSolution: For each period t, use I_t=I_{t-1}+p_t−d_t, together with I_0=10 and appropriate nonnegativity constraints.

commonWrongAnswers.explanation: Demand is an outflow and production is an inflow.

questions.prompt: Quantity x can be at most 80 and must be zero unless option y is selected. Write a linear link for binary y.

commonWrongAnswers.explanation: This permits positive x when y=0.

sourceReferences.fileName: CO 250 W26 IP and NLP formulation review.pdf

questions.prompt: For a maximization LP in SEF, list the checks needed for a base point x̄ and direction d to certify unboundedness.
hints: Separate feasibility of x̄ from the direction conditions.
solutionOutline: Check x̄ feasible; d≥0; Ad=0; cᵀd>0.
questions.fullSolution: Verify A x̄=b and x̄≥0. Then verify d≥0, Ad=0, and cᵀd>0. For every t≥0, x̄+td remains feasible and its objective grows without bound.

commonWrongAnswers.explanation: The direction must preserve Ax=b, so Ad=0.

questions.prompt: How is a free variable z represented using nonnegative variables in SEF?
questions.fullSolution: Replace z everywhere by z⁺−z⁻ with z⁺,z⁻≥0.

commonWrongAnswers.explanation: A sum of nonnegative variables cannot represent negative z.

questions.prompt: An entering column has positive entries 4 and 2 with right-hand sides 8 and 6. Which row leaves?
questions.fullSolution: The ratios are 8/4=2 and 6/2=3, so the basic variable in the first row leaves.

commonWrongAnswers.explanation: Simplex uses the minimum positive ratio.

questions.prompt: Under the course's negative-sum maximization convention, phase I ends with optimal value −3. What follows?
hints: Can all auxiliary variables be zero?
solutionOutline: A negative optimum means some auxiliary mass remains.
questions.fullSolution: The original LP is infeasible. If it were feasible, all auxiliary variables could be set to zero and phase I would attain value 0.

commonWrongAnswers.explanation: Phase II requires a phase-I optimum of 0.

questions.prompt: State the line-segment test for a convex set.
learningObjectives: State convexity with the correct quantifiers
solutionOutline: Require every convex combination to remain in S.
questions.fullSolution: S is convex if for every x,y∈S and every λ∈[0,1], λx+(1−λ)y∈S.

commonWrongAnswers.answer: Only the midpoint must lie in S

questions.prompt: A feasible primal maximization solution has value 12 and a feasible dual minimization solution has value 12. What can you conclude?
hints: Weak duality puts every primal value below every dual value.
solutionOutline: The matching bound leaves no gap.
questions.fullSolution: Both solutions are optimal, and the common optimal value is 12.

questions.prompt: True or false: in a complementary-slackness pair, exactly one of a primal variable and its dual slack must be zero.
learningObjectives: Interpret complementary slackness as inclusive OR
solutionOutline: The product must be zero; both factors may be zero.
questions.fullSolution: False. Complementary slackness requires at least one to be zero, and both may be zero.

questions.prompt: A maximization IP's LP relaxation has an integral optimal solution x*. What does this prove?
hints: The relaxation contains every IP-feasible point.
solutionOutline: x* is IP feasible and no IP point can beat the LP optimum.
questions.fullSolution: x* is optimal for the IP as well. It is integral and feasible, and the LP optimum upper-bounds every IP-feasible objective value.
questions.finalAnswer: x* is also optimal for the IP.

commonWrongAnswers.answer: It is only a lower bound
commonWrongAnswers.explanation: For maximization, the relaxation optimum is an upper bound.

questions.prompt: Before using the course KKT iff condition for min cᵀx subject to gᵢ(x)≤0, list the assumptions to check.
learningObjectives: Check KKT assumptions before applying the conclusion
hints: Check the functions, a strictly feasible point, and the candidate.
solutionOutline: Convexity, differentiability, Slater point, candidate feasibility.
questions.fullSolution: Check that every gᵢ is convex and differentiable, that a Slater point exists with every gᵢ(x)<0, and that the candidate x̄ is feasible. Then test whether −c is in the cone of gradients of the tight constraints.
questions.finalAnswer: Convex differentiable constraints, a Slater point, and candidate feasibility.

commonWrongAnswers.explanation: The iff theorem depends on the stated assumptions.
